input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the MABI MAX 100 serial robot with six axes, operating in a machining domain. Its main components include the first vertical robot axis at the base, motor, gear transmission, cycloidal gear, safety cell, and encoders. The robot is controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, which facilitates real-time data exchange for digital shadow creation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers monitoring services through continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction to enhance motion accuracy, and visualization services via an integrated dashboard that aggregates and displays data traces for real-time operational oversight by maintenance and engineering personnel.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation utilizes SysML for structural modeling of the robot's component & connector architecture (Fig. 3), MathWorks MATLAB Simulink for executable simulation models of friction in the first axis, and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for real-time data acquisition and integration with the digital shadow system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Automated one-way data flow from physical object to digital object as defined by Kritzinger et al. [32], with the digital shadow functioning as a passive entity that does not push data back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1) defines the architecture as a UML class diagram, with Asset Administration Shells (AAS) implementing standardized asset representation. Component integration includes AAS for data management and Fig. 5 detailing the concrete data model.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller via MQTT protocol for real-time data streaming, using Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for interoperability with enterprise systems and external control infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies with SysML for structural modeling (component & connector perspectives via IBDs), behavioral modeling through state machines in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc Automaton, and analytical modeling via MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java). Data sources include physical system telemetry processed via a digital shadow's one-way data flow, with heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives integrated across multiple modeling tools and notations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components consist of a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, coupled with a gear transmission and cycloidal gear mechanism for motion transmission. Control is executed via the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, which processes input commands to achieve precise actuation within specified mechanical limits, with safety constraints enforced through embedded firmware protocols.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive, with the motor encoder mounted directly on the motor and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox to mitigate backlash and reduce positional uncertainty. Sensor data is collected as time series streams with real-time transmission to the Digital Twin system, ensuring high-frequency monitoring for dynamic system state tracking.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Fidelity and validity are maintained through rigorous validation of simulated data against measured data, with deviation analysis quantifying accuracy. Friction models are calibrated to maximize precision, and positional uncertainty is systematically assessed. Validation processes compare reference values against simulated outputs, while error detection mechanisms identify discrepancies in motion accuracy to ensure model reliability.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data (e.g., input current, output angle) is streamed via MQTT protocol; preprocessing steps include deriving torque, angular velocity, and acceleration from raw inputs.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication occurs via MQTT protocol for sensor data streaming; network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Industry standards and specifications include SysML [17], AADL [24], ACME [23], MontiArc [25,26], RFLP [18,19], OOSEM [20], SPES [21], and SMArDT [22], with simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink [28] and general-purpose programming languages like C++ or Java for detailed behavior modeling.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the MABI MAX 100 as a six-axis serial robot used for machining. It correctly lists key components (first vertical axis, motor, gear transmission, cycloidal gear, safety cell, encoders) and the controller (Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl). This is strongly supported by Section III, which describes the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and its components, and mentions the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' controller.

The extraction describes monitoring services via 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' and visualization services via an 'integrated dashboard'. This aligns with the abstract and Section IV, which state the digital shadow enables 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' and includes 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard'. However, the specific mention of 'target users' (maintenance and engineering personnel) is not explicitly supported by the source text.

The extraction correctly identifies SysML for structural modeling (Section IV.A mentions a 'component & connector SysML model'), MathWorks MATLAB Simulink for executable simulation models (Section IV.A states 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'), and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for real-time data acquisition (Section III mentions the controller and its capabilities). The roles of these tools are accurately described based on the text.

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering

The document mentions 'real-time' and 'live measurements' but provides no specific temporal specifications. The extraction provides information about data flow direction, not time scale.

The document describes a single digital shadow instance for a specific robot axis and does not discuss multiple instances, hierarchical structures, or decentralized architectures. Therefore, the extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1)' as a 'UML class diagram' and mentions 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' as part of the implementation, which aligns with the Abstract ('implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)'). It also accurately cites 'Fig. 5 detailing the concrete data model'. However, the text provided does not explicitly link Fig. 1 to AAS implementation in the 'Background' section; AAS is mentioned in the Abstract and Introduction. The extraction provides a good high-level summary of the architecture but slightly conflates the reference model description with the specific technology stack (AAS). It captures the key components (UML diagram, AAS, Concrete model) effectively.

The extraction accurately describes the integration with the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller' and the use of the 'MQTT protocol' for data streaming, which is supported by the text: 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl... time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'. It also correctly links 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' to interoperability, consistent with the Abstract's mention of 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)'. The extraction is specific, technically accurate, and well-supported by the provided snippets.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly detailed and perfectly aligns with the source text. It correctly identifies the use of MBSE and SysML for structural modeling (Internal Block Diagrams are mentioned in the text). It accurately captures the behavioral modeling tools cited: 'MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7' and analytical modeling via 'MathWorks Simulink' and 'general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'. The extraction also correctly describes the data flow as a 'one-way data flow' characteristic of a digital shadow and notes the integration of 'various heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives'. The extraction correctly identifies the motor type, gear transmission, cycloidal gear, and the Siemens SINUMERIK controller, supported by the text describing the first axis. It accurately mentions the mechanical limits (range and speed) and safety constraints. However, the phrase 'safety constraints enforced through embedded firmware protocols' is not supported; the text attributes the range restriction to the 'safety cell of the robot'. Quotes: 'first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission... followed by a cycloidal gear', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The description is excellent and fully grounded in the text, detailing the specific sensor arrangement (two encoders), their locations (motor and behind gearbox), and their purpose (reducing backlash/uncertainty). It also correctly captures the data handling mechanism (time series via MQTT). Quotes: 'There are two position encoders per drive', 'motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox', 'access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'.

The extraction accurately summarizes the methodology described in Section IV.A, specifically the comparison of simulated vs. measured data to understand friction and deviation. It effectively maps the text's description of the digital shadow's purpose (deriving friction loss via deviation analysis) to concepts of validation and calibration. While terms like 'error detection mechanisms' are not explicit in the text, the extraction is a strong interpretation of the stated goal to compare reference and simulated values. Quotes: 'purpose... is the deviation of simulated data versus measured data', 'reference values and simulated values which we compare against the reference values', 'continuously improving both white- and black-box friction models'.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text discusses modeling and digital shadow creation but does not mention lifecycle phases, transitions between phases, or lifecycle governance approaches relevant to a Digital Twin's lifecycle. No evidence supports this characteristic.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the paper describes a 'methodology on how to create Digital Shadows' (Section IV.A) with steps like problem identification and model analysis, it does not discuss testing, validation, version control, or evolution management strategies for the Digital Twin or twinning process. The provided methodology is limited to creating a digital shadow for a specific purpose, not the broader engineering or evolution management of the DT itself. Thus, the specific details required by the characteristic description are absent.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The paper mentions that the digital shadow enables 'understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss' and 'improve robot control’s accuracy', which implies decision support. However, the description requires details on 'types of insights generated, analytics and processing capabilities, decision making processes, user interaction methods, and confidence measures'. The source mentions 'visualization in a dashboard' and 'understanding why the data points have changed', but lacks detail on specific analytics, user interaction methods beyond visualization, or confidence measures. The extraction correctly identifies that the *detailed* characteristic description is not met, although high-level intent exists. Given the instruction 'If "Not in Document" is stated and no evidence exists in the sources, assign a 5', and the specific details are missing, a score of 5 is appropriate.

The extraction lists specific industry standards (SysML, AADL, etc.) and tools (Simulink, C++). The Source Documents confirm the use of SysML for modeling the robot structure ('SysML model describing the first axis of the robot') and explicitly state: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML [17]... RFLP [18], [19], OOSEM [20], SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]... ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26]... MathWorks Simulink [28]... code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java.' The extraction accurately captures the standards and tools mentioned in the background section, directly matching the source text.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The provided text focuses on the technical implementation of digital shadows, friction modeling, and data traceability for robot motion control. It does not contain any information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy regulations, consent management, or data sharing agreements. The extraction correctly identifies this as missing.

While the text mentions a physical 'safety cell' restricting the robot's range, it does not describe cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or fail-safe mechanisms for the digital twin or data handling system. The description 'Not in Document' is correct regarding the defined characteristic scope.
